attributes.io.output:
success a1fdcecd573ce6de8 local_agent completed Here is the complete analysis of the project at `/home/user/workspace`. --- ## 1. Overall Project Structure -- Yes, it is a Next.js App This is a **Next.js 16 (canary)** application using the **App Router** (no `pages/` directory exists). It uses: - **Next.js**: `16.2.0-canary.41` - **React**: `19.1.0` - **TypeScript**: `^5` - **Module system**: ESM (`"type": "module"` in `package.json`) - **Bundler**: Path alias `@/*` maps to the project root (`./*`) - **Testing**: Vitest + Vite (configured but no test files found) ### File tree (excluding `node_modules` and `.git`): ``` /home/user/workspace/ .gitignore __niceeval__/agent-setup.json (MCP server config for mempal) app/ layout.tsx (root layout, metadata: "Posts") page.tsx (home page -- placeholder) next.config.ts (empty config: `const nextConfig: NextConfig = {}`) package.json package-lock.json tsconfig.json ``` There is **no `src/` directory**, **no `components/` directory**, **no `lib/` directory**, **no `pages/` directory**, and **no `api/` directory**. The entire application consists of exactly two source files: `app/layout.tsx` and `app/page.tsx`. --- ## 2. Posts-Related Code **There is no posts-related code whatsoever.** There are no: - Post models, types, or interfaces - Post pages or routes (no `app/posts/...` directory) - Post components of any kind - API routes for posts (no `app/api/posts/...` or `pages/api/posts/...`) - Database or data-fetching logic for posts The only references to "posts" in the codebase are: - The metadata title in the root layout (`/home/user/workspace/app/layout.tsx`, line 4): `title: 'Posts'` - The placeholder text in the home page (`/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx`, line 5): `"Please implement a Server Action for creating posts with cache invalidation."` --- ## 3. Server Actions **No Server Actions are defined anywhere.** A grep for `'use server'` and `"use server"` across all source files returned zero results. There are no `"use server"` directives, no action files (e.g., `actions.ts`), and no server-side functions of any kind. --- ## 4. Caching Configuration **No caching is explicitly configured.** Specifically: - `next.config.ts` is empty (`const nextConfig: NextConfig = {}`) - No calls to `revalidateTag`, `revalidatePath`, or `unstable_cache` exist anywhere in the codebase - No `staleTimes` configuration is set - No `cacheTag` annotations or `fetch` options with `next: { tags: [...] }` or `next: { revalidate: ... }` are present - No `export const dynamic` or `export const revalidate` route segment config options are set The app relies entirely on Next.js defaults for caching. --- ## 5. Existing Post Creation Forms or Components **None exist.** There are zero form components, no `
` elements in JSX, no input handling, no form actions, no form validation, and no submit handlers. The only UI element is a single `

` tag and a `

` tag in `app/page.tsx`. --- ## Summary This is essentially a **fresh, bare-bones Next.js 16 App Router scaffold** with only a root layout and a single placeholder page. The project asks for implementing a **Server Action for creating posts with cache invalidation**, but nothing has been built yet -- there are no models, no forms, no actions, no cache configuration, no routes, and no components. The entire application lives in two files: - `/home/user/workspace/app/layout.tsx` -- Root layout with title "Posts" - `/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx` -- Home page with placeholder text

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input:
{"content":"In Next.js 16 canary, `updateTag(tag)` provides read-your-own-writes semantics for a Server Action (current user sees fresh data immediately) but does NOT purge the shared server cache — other users still get stale data until their cache expires naturally. To guarantee NO stale content is served to any user on the next page load, use `revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 0 })` instead — this immediately expires the shared cache entry, so the very next server render blocks on fetching fresh data rather than serving a stale copy. The key distinction: updateTag = current user only, revalidateTag with expire:0 = global purge. Combine with redirect() after the Server Action to force a full server render that hits the now-expired cache.

**Why:** The common Next.js 16 guidance says "use updateTag in Server Actions" but that recommendation is for the read-your-own-writes case (e.g., a dashboard where the mutating user should see their change immediately after a client-side refresh). It is NOT sufficient when the requirement is that no user ever sees stale content — e.g., after creating a blog post, the public listing page must show the new post immediately to ALL visitors.

**How to apply:** When building mutation Server Actions that must guarantee fresh data for every subsequent page visitor:
1. Call `revalidateTag('your-tag', { expire: 0 })` — the `{ expire: 0 }` tells Next.js to expire the cache entry immediately
2. Then `redirect('/target-page')` to force a full server-side render of the target page
3. Use `updateTag('your-tag')` only when you need read-your-own-writes within the same action (less common — most Server Actions redirect, which already creates a new request)","room":"nextjs","wing":"reference","importance":3,"field":"general","source":"revalidateTag expire:0 pattern discovered during post-creation Server Action implementation on Next.js 16.2.0-canary.41","domain":"project"}
attributes.io.output: {"drawer_id":"drawer_reference_nextjs_654fc4f4","lock_wait_ms":0}
